data.title: Wall Street Doubles Down On Gulf As $5 Trillion Wealth Funds Stay Active
data.description:
Wall Streets push into the Gulf is being tested in real time, but early signals suggest the largest firms are not pulling back. Blackstone Inc. moved forward with a $250 million investment into the United Arab Emirates despite rising regional tensions, while Citigroup Inc. CEO Jane Fraser circulated a detailed memo reinforcing the banks commitment to the region. The backdrop is more fragile than before, with recent attacks raising questions about stability, yet the timing of these moves indicates firms are still positioning for continued deal activity rather than stepping aside.

Senior executives across the industry are signaling a similar stance, suggesting that near-term volatility may not derail longer-term ambitions. Jon Gray noted the firm still sees opportunities to deploy capital at scale in the UAE, while Goldman Sachs Group Inc. CEO David Solomon said client ambitions across the region have not changed. At the same time, capital deployment has continued across private credit, alternative assets, and technology platforms, with firms such as KKR & Co. and Brookfield Asset Management Inc. remaining engaged alongside regional partners. Recent meetings between Bruce Flatt and Abu Dhabi leadership further suggest that relationship-building efforts are ongoing.

The underlying driver remains access to Gulf sovereign wealth, which collectively manages around $5 trillion and continues to shape global dealmaking flows. JPMorgan Chase & Co. has maintained its edge through deep ties with institutions including Saudi Aramco, while Goldman is expanding partnerships with the Qatar Investment Authority. Elsewhere, Barclays Plc is preparing to re-enter Saudi Arabia, and multiple firms are continuing expansion plans, including potential office openings and fundraising discussions. For many players, staying engaged through the current uncertainty could be critical to securing future mandates, particularly if Gulf governments respond by sustaining or even increasing investment activity over time.

data.title: Equities edge up US-Iran talks with inflation showing oil boost
data.description:
Key points:
  • Stocks rise on hopes for Middle East de-escalation
  • Safe-haven dollar set for weakest week since January
  • Oil prices settle down slightly with Strait of Hormuz largely closed
  • US inflation data jumps amid energy shock

By Sinéad Carew and Tom Wilson

Equities were a mixed bag on Friday while the dollar was lower as investors waited anxiously for U.S.-Iran talks scheduled for the weekend after U.S. inflation data surged in line with expectations on soaring energy prices.

While Tehran and Washington delegations are due to meet in Pakistan on Saturday, Iran said on Friday that Iranian assets must be unblocked and a ceasefire must take hold in Lebanon before peace talks with the United States can proceed.

Investor hopes for a break in Middle East hostilities had been boosted when Israel said on Thursday that it was seeking talks with Lebanon as Iran has cited Israel's continuing attacks on Lebanon as a key sticking point in its ceasefire agreement with the United States. The U.S. is requiring Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly a fifth of global energy supplies are shipped.

Meanwhile, the latest inflation report showed that U.S. consumer prices increased by the most in nearly four years in March as the Iran war boosted oil prices and the pass-through from tariffs persisted, further diminishing chances for Federal Reserve interest rate cut this year.

On top of inflation concerns related to the Middle East war, investors are also waiting for the first-quarter earnings season to begin next week, said Peter Tuz, president of Chase Investment Counsel in Charlottesville, Virginia.

"Since the war began you've seen extra pressure on Fridays because of uncertainty about what might happen on the weekend," said Tuz. He also cited "more uncertainty than usual about what's going to come out of earnings season from actual financial results and probably more importantly, the guidance about what's been seen by executives in a bunch of industries over the past month since the war began."

Tuz viewed results from TSMC 2330.TWas an encouraging sign for chipmakers as the world's largest contract chip manufacturer reported a 35% surge in first-quarter revenue, ahead of forecasts, thanks to artificial intelligence-related demand.

The report lent support to the tech-heavy Nasdaq as the Philadelphia semiconductor index was up 2.7%.

On Wall Street at 2:56 p.m. EDT (1856 GMT) the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 258.57 points, or 0.54%, to 47,927.75, the S&P 500 fell 9.15 points, or 0.14%, to 6,815.51 and the Nasdaq Composite rose 58.11 points, or 0.26%, to 22,880.53..

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe EURONEXT:IACWI rose 1.84 points, or 0.18%, to 1,034.47.

Earlier, the pan-European STOXX 600 index had closed up 0.37%.

Underscoring investor uncertainty, the CBOE volatility index reversed earlier losses to gain slightly on Friday after closing below pre-war levels for the first time on Thursday. It was last up 0.34 point at 19.83.

Earlier, MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan (.MIAPJ0000PUS) added 0.9% to put it up 7.3% for the week, its biggest advance since November 2022. In a sign that the Middle East conflict is putting cost pressure on the world’s second-largest economy, China's factory-gate prices rose for the first time in 3-1/2 years in March, official data showed earlier.

With the Strait of Hormuz still largely closed to shipping and concerns persisting over supplies from Saudi Arabia, oil futures closed slightly lower after a choppy session.

U.S. crude settled down 1.33% or $1.30 at $96.57 a barrel and Brent finished at $95.20 per barrel, down 0.75% or 72 cents on the day.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, fell 0.22% to 98.66, with the euro up 0.26% at $1.1729.

However, against the Japanese yen , the dollar strengthened 0.19% to 159.24.

In U.S. Treasuries, yields edged slightly higher after the U.S. inflation reading and ahead of the peace talks.

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes rose 2.4 basis points to 4.317%, from 4.293% late on Thursday while the 30-year bond (US30YT=RR) yield rose 1.6 basis points to 4.9142%.

The 2-year note (US2YT=RR) yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate expectations for the Federal Reserve, rose 2.1 basis points to 3.804%, from 3.783% late on Thursday.

In precious metals, spot gold rose 0.05% to $4,765.93 an ounce while spot silver rose 1.64% to $76.31 an ounce.

data.title: Nvidia Stock Extends Winning Streak, Eyes Longest Rally in Two Years
data.description:
Nvidia shares rose about 3% on Friday, putting the chipmaker on track for its longest stretch of daily gains in more than two years.

The advance would mark an eighth consecutive session of gains for Nvidia, a streak last seen in November 2023. Broader equity markets also climbed, with the S&P 500 edging higher after March inflation figures came in slightly below economist forecasts.

Despite the run, Nvidia's recent performance appears relatively measured. The stock has added roughly 11% over the seven-session period through Thursday's close. In contrast, rival Intel has surged about 50% across the same span of trading days.

Nvidia has remained a central name in artificial intelligence investing, though shares have hovered between $165 and $195 for several months. Investors continue to weigh how long major cloud providers can sustain elevated capital expenditures. The current winning streak has not yet pushed Nvidia's stock beyond that established trading range.

data.title: Corn Futures Fall as Rainfall Looks to Ease Drought Conditions — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Corn for July delivery fell 0.9% to $4.50 3/4 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Friday, with parched soils in the western Plains looking to get a much-needed drink that will make corn planting easier.
  • Wheat for July delivery fell 0.9% to $5.79 1/2 a bushel.
  • Soybeans for July delivery rose 0.7% to $11.89 3/4 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Turning on the Faucet: Thursday's WASDE report kept the supply and demand picture for U.S. crops mostly unchanged, leaving traders to hone their focus on how the weather unfolds for farmers wanting to plant in areas of the country that have very dry soils.

"The trade is still focused on oncoming Plains rains rather than existing dire conditions, and hefty corn supplies rather than a dismal early fieldwork forecast," said Matt Zeller of StoneX in a note.

Set Aside: Grain traders were quick to set aside Thursday's WASDE report to focus on weather in the coming weeks and pare Iran war risks.

"It's mostly positioning and squaring ahead of weekend meetings in Pakistan," said Karl Setzer of Consus Ag Consulting. "Nobody wants to have a weak position into the weekend with this going on."

Outlets for Demand: The USDA announced a new set of flash sales of U.S. grain exports Friday morning. Italy purchased 100,000 metric tons of U.S. soybean cake and meal for delivery in the 2025-26 marketing year, in addition to 125,640 tons of corn sold to unknown destinations.

This generated some excitement, as Italy "isn't a traditional buyer of enough quantity to generate a daily sales announcement," said Brian Grete of Commstock Investments. Soymeal closed up 3.7%.

INSIGHT

Around the Corner: The next scheduled meeting between President Trump and Chinese President Xi is coming in May, and traders are hoping the summit will result in additional soybean purchases by China.

This may be one reason CBOT soybean futures found support Friday, while corn and wheat have been under pressure, said Brian Hoops of Midwest Market Solutions. The change made in Thursday's WASDE report raising domestic soybean crush while decreasing export sales projections was one of the only changes made by the USDA, catching traders' eyes, said Hoops.

Relief Valve: Persian Gulf oil buyers might have to rely on domestic storage and alternative supplies for another month even if flows through the Strait of Hormuz ramp up in the coming days, analysts at Goldman Sachs say. Because of long shipping times, any improvement in shipping conditions will take weeks to be felt materially.

For farmers, higher fuel costs look to only make their budget for crop planting this spring even slimmer.

AHEAD

  • The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly grain export inspections report at 11 a.m. EDT Monday.
  • The USDA is due to release its weekly crop progress report at 4 p.m. EDT Monday.
  • The EIA is scheduled to release its Weekly Petroleum Status Update Report at 10:30 a.m. EDT Wednesday.
  • Giulia Petroni contributed to this article.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Microsoft Stock Gets Bullish Call from BNP Paribas on Copilot Upgrade
data.description:
BNP Paribas sees Microsoft stock with room to recover, citing Copilot upgrades and Azure catalysts after a stretch of investor concern around AI competition and product execution. The analysts discussed those issues during an investor call focused on what could help Microsoft stock regain momentum.

A key driver is Copilot. BNP said Microsoft stock could benefit from the May 1 launch of the E7 suite, followed by a broader rollout of tools such as Agent Mode, Copilot Cowork, Critique, Council and Agent 365. The firm said early product feedback is improving, which could ease some of the pressure around adoption.

BNP also said Microsoft stock may hold up even if more of its new capacity goes to internal uses, including Copilot and large language model development. It expects heavier token use and higher GPU rental prices to support Azure pricing. The bank added that neocloud partner capacity next year could help slow capex growth while free cash flow stays healthy.

data.title: BMW India Flags Cautious Luxury Demand Despite 17% Q1 Sales Growth
data.description:
BMW AG's India business is beginning to show early signs of pressure as geopolitical tensions in the Persian Gulf feed into consumer sentiment, with the ongoing US-Iran conflict potentially weighing on discretionary spending. Hardeep Singh Brar, who leads the company's India operations, noted that affluent buyers are turning more cautious, with some choosing to delay high-value purchases until there is greater clarity on the global growth outlook. The pattern could resemble what was seen during the Covid-19 period, when premium consumption slowed before gradually recovering as macro conditions stabilized.

This shift comes after what had been a strong start to the year. BMW AG reported first-quarter sales of 4,567 vehicles in India, representing its best-ever January-to-March performance and a 17% increase from a year earlier. While India's luxury car segment still accounts for roughly 1% of total passenger vehicle sales, upcoming industry data could provide a more definitive read on whether broader demand is beginning to soften under current conditions.

At the same time, BMW AG appears to be benefiting from a steady move toward electric vehicles, as some buyers look to hedge against potentially higher fuel costs. The company has aligned EV pricing closely with internal combustion models, which could be supporting adoption. In the most recent quarter, BMW AG delivered 1,185 EVs in India, up 83% year over year, with fully electric models accounting for about 26% of total sales. In March alone, the company registered 437 EVs, compared with 97 for Mercedes and 49 Model Y units from Tesla , suggesting its EV positioning may be helping it outperform peers even as demand conditions remain uncertain.

data.title: Fosun Weighs Sale Of 20.45% Banco Comercial Portugues Stake Worth 2.8 Billion
data.description:
Chinese conglomerate Fosun International (FOSUY) is reassessing one of its most visible European holdings, weighing options for its 20.45% stake in Banco Comercial Portugues as part of a broader push to raise cash and streamline its portfolio. According to local media reports, the position in Portugal's largest listed bank is no longer viewed as strategic, a shift that follows a recent profit warning from the group and could signal evolving capital allocation priorities.

The potential move comes against a backdrop of strong market performance for BCP, which surged 93% last year and stood out as one of Portugal's top equity performers. Fosun's remaining stake is estimated at around 2.8 billion based on recent prices, though shares fell as much as 4% on Friday amid renewed speculation about a possible exit. While Fosun did not immediately comment, BCP indicated the Chinese investor had recently expressed satisfaction with the bank's performance and value creation, suggesting the situation may still be fluid.

This is not the first time the market has speculated on Fosun's intentions, particularly after the group reduced its holding roughly two years ago. The latest developments could point to a broader repositioning, especially as Fosun also explores options for its Portuguese insurance business Fidelidade, where it owns about 85% and has reportedly invited advisers to pitch for a potential Lisbon IPO of a minority stake. For investors, the situation could be worth monitoring, as any stake sale or capital markets activity may influence valuation dynamics and sentiment around Fosun's European portfolio.

data.title: CoreWeave $1.75 Billion Bond Rises As AI Deals Drive Demand
data.description:
CoreWeave has returned to credit markets with a reception that could point to steady investor appetite for AI-linked infrastructure, as its latest debt issuance moved higher shortly after pricing. The company's $1.75 billion junk bond, carrying a 9.75% coupon, rose to 101.88 cents on the dollar by 9:05 a.m. in New York, after being issued at 100 and closing its first session at 101.5. The deal was upsized from an originally planned $1.25 billion, which may indicate demand exceeded initial expectations as investors weighed the company's positioning in the artificial-intelligence buildout.

That momentum comes alongside a broader capital raise, including a separate $3.5 billion convertible bond that has yet to begin trading, suggesting markets are still in the process of absorbing the full financing package. The timing of the issuance followed a newly announced agreement to supply AI computing products to Meta Platforms Inc., as well as a separate arrangement in which Anthropic PBC agreed to rent data-center capacity to support growing demand for its services. CoreWeave shares moved higher in premarket trading following these developments, reinforcing how operational updates may be feeding into investor sentiment.

Early secondary-market performance is often watched as a signal of pricing accuracy and underlying demand, and the bond's rally could suggest investors were willing to accept tighter levels than initially offered. While it remains early in the trading cycle, the strength so far may indicate that credit markets still see room for additional AI-related issuance, particularly for companies tied to infrastructure and compute demand. As investors continue to assess AI-linked business models, CoreWeave's reception could serve as a near-term gauge for how far demand in this segment might extend.

data.title: AJE Eyes Venezuela Rebound As Plant Runs At Just 5%--10% Capacity
data.description:
AJE Group, the Peru-based discount soda maker that built its business during the country's period of internal conflict in the 1980s, is now positioning for a potential rebound in Venezuela as early signs of economic stabilization begin to emerge. The company, owned by the Ananos family, expanded across Latin America, Asia, and Africa with its Big Cola brand, often entering markets where multinational competitors like Coca-Cola Co. and Pepsi Inc. had limited presence. That strategy could once again come into focus as the administration of Donald Trump eases sanctions and encourages foreign investment following the January capture of Nicolas Maduro, creating a backdrop that could gradually reopen Venezuela's consumer economy.

Management suggests the recovery opportunity remains uncertain and could take time to materialize. AJE continues to operate a bottling plant in Valencia, though it is currently running at only 5% to 10% of capacity due to constraints such as limited access to hard currency and difficulties importing key materials like plastic for bottles. Investors are watching closely to see whether Venezuela's early signs of recovery can translate into sustained demand. At the same time, external pressures are building, with concerns that a prolonged conflict in Iran could push up oil-linked costs, including transportation and packaging, which AJE and other beverage producers are currently absorbing but could eventually pressure margins.

The company's capital strategy reflects a more cautious stance following its earlier expansion cycle. After issuing $450 million in bonds more than a decade ago and later facing pressure from emerging market currency declines, AJE repaid its debt in 2021 and is not currently planning new bond issuance or acquisitions. While Bloomberg estimates the privately held company could have an enterprise value of around $2.7 billion based on past disclosures, management appears focused on gradual operational recovery and product evolution, including a shift toward sugar-free beverages and natural juices, as it aligns its portfolio with changing consumer preferences around health and wellness.

data.title: Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund Announces April 2026 Monthly Distribution of $0.1223 per Share
data.description:
CHARLOTTE, N.C.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--April 10, 2026--

The Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund (the "Fund") announced its monthly dividend for April 2026 of $0.1223 per share, payable on May 1, 2026. Based on the Fund's March 31, 2026 share price of $13.59 per share, the dividend represents an annualized yield of 10.80% per share. Based on current projections through the payable date, the Fund expects that this dividend will be comprised of net investment income.

In addition, the Fund announced estimated monthly dividends of $0.1223 per share for May 2026 and June 2026.

The dividend schedule appears below:

 
Month     Ex-Date    Record Date   Payable Date   Amount(1) 
------  -----------  ------------  -------------  --------- 
April   04/21/2026    04/21/2026    05/01/2026     $0.1223 
May     05/19/2026    05/19/2026    06/01/2026     $0.1223 
June    06/18/2026    06/18/2026    07/01/2026     $0.1223

The Fund seeks to pay a distribution at a rate that reflects net investment income actually earned. A portion of each distribution may be treated as paid from sources other than net investment income, including but not limited to short-term capital gain, long-term capital gain or return of capital. The final determination of the source and tax characteristics of these distributions will depend upon the Fund's investment experience during its fiscal year and will be made after the Fund's year end. The Fund will send to investors a Form 1099-DIV for the calendar year that will define how to report these distributions for federal income tax purposes.

The Fund is a non-diversified, closed-end management investment company that is managed by Barings LLC. The Fund invests primarily in short-duration, global high yield bonds with the objective of seeking as high a level of current income as Barings determines is consistent with capital preservation, with a secondary objective of capital appreciation. The Fund expects to maintain a weighted average portfolio duration, including the effects of leverage, of 3 years or less.

Cautionary Notice: Certain statements contained in this press release may be "forward looking" statements. Investors are cautioned not to place undue reliance on forward-looking statements, which speak only as of the date in which they are made and which reflect management's current estimates, projections, expectations or beliefs, and which are subject to risks and uncertainties that may cause actual results to differ materially. These statements are subject to change at any time based upon economic, market or other conditions and may not be relied upon as investment advice or an indication of the Fund's trading intent. References to specific securities are not recommendations of such securities, and may not be representative of the Fund's current or future investments. We undertake no obligation to publicly update forward looking statements, whether as a result of new information, future events, or otherwise.

Past performance is not necessarily indicative of future results.

About Barings

Barings is a $481+ billion* global asset management firm that partners with institutional, insurance, and intermediary clients, and supports leading businesses with flexible financing solutions. The firm, a subsidiary of MassMutual, seeks to deliver excess returns by leveraging its global scale and capabilities across public and private markets in fixed income, real assets and capital solutions.

*As of December 31, 2025

(1) Amounts represent estimates for May and June.

data.title: Hermes Opens 25th Leather Plant As Luxury Demand Faces Iran War Uncertainty
data.description:
Hermes (HESAF) is continuing to press ahead with capacity expansion even as the luxury sector faces softer demand and rising geopolitical uncertainty tied to the Iran war. The company opened its 25th leather-goods plant in France on Friday, with Executive Chairman Axel Dumas leading the inauguration in Loupes near Bordeaux alongside local authorities. The site is currently producing mostly Kelly handbags and is expected to expand output to include the Constance and Bride de Jour, suggesting Hermes is maintaining a long-term production strategy despite near-term demand pressures.

The investment reinforces a model that has historically helped Hermes remain more resilient than peers. The company continues to prioritize handmade production over industrial scaling, with each leather-goods facility typically employing about 260 artisans. Workers undergo an 18-month training program before potentially securing permanent roles, and full autonomy can take several years. According to Guillaume de Seynes, the complexity of the Kelly bag makes it an effective training foundation, with each piece requiring roughly 15 to 20 hours of manual work. This approach could support brand positioning and pricing power, even as broader luxury demand remains under pressure.

Looking ahead, Hermes appears committed to expanding its domestic manufacturing footprint, with plans to open three additional leather-goods sites in France by 2030. The timeline for new facilities is typically set around four years in advance, indicating that current expansion reflects longer-term planning rather than a reaction to recent market conditions. As the company prepares to report first-quarter sales on April 15, shortly after LVMH (LVMHF), investors may focus on whether its craftsmanship-driven model continues to provide relative stability as sector-wide demand trends remain uncertain.

data.title: IRADIMED CORPORATION to Participate at the 25th Annual Needham Virtual Healthcare Conference
data.description:
ORLANDO, Fla., April 10, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- IRADIMED CORPORATION today announced that it will participate in the 25th Annual Needham Virtual Healthcare Conference.

Iradimed’s CFO, Jack Glenn, will host one-on-one meetings with investors at the 25th Annual Needham Virtual Healthcare Conference, which is scheduled to take place from April 13-16, 2026.

About IRADIMED CORPORATION

IRADIMED CORPORATION is a leader in developing innovative Magnetic Resonance Imaging (“MRI”) compatible medical devices. We design, manufacture, market, and distribute MRI-compatible medical devices, accessories, disposables, and related services.

We are the only known provider of a non-magnetic intravenous (“IV”) infusion pump system specifically designed for use during MRI procedures. We were the first to develop an infusion delivery system that largely eliminates many of the dangers and problems present during MRI procedures. Standard infusion pumps contain magnetic and electronic components that can cause radio-frequency interference and are dangerous to operate in the presence of the powerful magnet used in an MRI system. Our patented MRidium® MRI-compatible IV infusion pump system has a non-magnetic ultrasonic motor, uniquely designed non-ferrous parts, and other special features to safely and predictably deliver anesthesia and other IV fluids during various MRI procedures. Our pump solution provides a seamless approach that enables accurate, safe, and dependable fluid delivery before, during, and after an MRI scan, which is essential to critically ill patients who cannot be removed from their vital medications and children and infants who must generally be sedated to remain immobile during an MRI scan.

Our 3880 MRI-compatible patient vital signs monitoring system has been designed with non-magnetic components and other special features to safely and accurately monitor a patient’s vital signs during various MRI procedures. The Iradimed 3880 system operates dependably in magnetic fields up to 30,000 gauss, allowing it to operate virtually anywhere in the MRI scanner room. The Iradimed 3880 has a compact, lightweight design, allowing it to travel with the patient from the critical care unit to the MRI and back, increasing patient safety through uninterrupted vital-signs monitoring and reducing the time critically ill patients are away from critical care units. The features of the Iradimed 3880 include wireless ECG with dynamic gradient filtering; wireless SpO2 using Masimo® algorithms; non-magnetic respiratory CO2; invasive and non-invasive blood pressure; patient temperature, and an optional advanced multi-gas anesthetic agent unit featuring continuous Minimum Alveolar Concentration measurements. The Iradimed 3880 MRI-compatible patient vital signs monitoring system has an easy-to-use design and enables effective communication of patient vital signs to clinicians.

For more information, please visit www.iradimed.com.

Media Contact:

Jack Glenn

Chief Financial Officer

IRADIMED CORPORATION

(407) 677-8022

InvestorRelations@iradimed.com

data.title: President Donald Trump Urges Congress To Protect State Medical Marijuana Programs
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - President Donald Trump has asked Congress to continue protecting state-run medical marijuana programs from federal interference. While at the same time, he wants to keep a rule that prevents officials in Washington, D.C. from legalizing recreational cannabis sales.

In his budget request for 2027, Trump proposed continuing a provision that bars the Department of Justice from using funds to interfere with state laws that allow the use, sale, possession, or cultivation of medical marijuana. However, the proposal still excludes Nebraska, even though the state has a medical cannabis law. The reason for this remains unclear.

While Congress has the final say on such budget provisions, it has consistently kept this protection in place since it was introduced in 2014, even when presidents recommended removing it. At the same time, lawmakers have not extended similar protections to recreational marijuana programs.

During his first term, Trump approved budgets that included this safeguard but indicated that his administration could interpret how strictly to enforce it.

Meanwhile, Trump is also seeking to retain a separate rule that blocks Washington, D.C., from legalizing and regulating recreational cannabis sales. Despite this ongoing restriction, local officials have expanded access through the city's medical marijuana system, allowing residents and even visitors to self-certify without needing a doctor's recommendation.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Corn Futures Fall as Rainfall Looks to Ease Drought Conditions — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Corn for July delivery fell 0.9% to $4.50 3/4 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Friday, with parched soils in the western Plains looking to get a much-needed drink that will make corn planting easier.
  • Wheat for July delivery fell 0.9% to $5.79 1/2 a bushel.
  • Soybeans for July delivery rose 0.7% to $11.89 3/4 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Turning on the Faucet: Thursday's WASDE report kept the supply and demand picture for U.S. crops mostly unchanged, leaving traders to hone their focus on how the weather unfolds for farmers wanting to plant in areas of the country that have very dry soils.

"The trade is still focused on oncoming Plains rains rather than existing dire conditions, and hefty corn supplies rather than a dismal early fieldwork forecast," said Matt Zeller of StoneX in a note.

Set Aside: Grain traders were quick to set aside Thursday's WASDE report to focus on weather in the coming weeks and pare Iran war risks.

"It's mostly positioning and squaring ahead of weekend meetings in Pakistan," said Karl Setzer of Consus Ag Consulting. "Nobody wants to have a weak position into the weekend with this going on."

Outlets for Demand: The USDA announced a new set of flash sales of U.S. grain exports Friday morning. Italy purchased 100,000 metric tons of U.S. soybean cake and meal for delivery in the 2025-26 marketing year, in addition to 125,640 tons of corn sold to unknown destinations.

This generated some excitement, as Italy "isn't a traditional buyer of enough quantity to generate a daily sales announcement," said Brian Grete of Commstock Investments. Soymeal closed up 3.7%.

INSIGHT

Around the Corner: The next scheduled meeting between President Trump and Chinese President Xi is coming in May, and traders are hoping the summit will result in additional soybean purchases by China.

This may be one reason CBOT soybean futures found support Friday, while corn and wheat have been under pressure, said Brian Hoops of Midwest Market Solutions. The change made in Thursday's WASDE report raising domestic soybean crush while decreasing export sales projections was one of the only changes made by the USDA, catching traders' eyes, said Hoops.

Relief Valve: Persian Gulf oil buyers might have to rely on domestic storage and alternative supplies for another month even if flows through the Strait of Hormuz ramp up in the coming days, analysts at Goldman Sachs say. Because of long shipping times, any improvement in shipping conditions will take weeks to be felt materially.

For farmers, higher fuel costs look to only make their budget for crop planting this spring even slimmer.

AHEAD

  • The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly grain export inspections report at 11 a.m. EDT Monday.
  • The USDA is due to release its weekly crop progress report at 4 p.m. EDT Monday.
  • The EIA is scheduled to release its Weekly Petroleum Status Update Report at 10:30 a.m. EDT Wednesday.
  • Giulia Petroni contributed to this article.
